constraints.text: Potential physical interferents (such as leaks of the sampling system or introduction of foreign material like particulate matter, insect nests, spider webs) could impact the ability to properly time-integrate the sampling.

Failure to adhere to the cleaning and certification requirements included in this method may lead to interference issues, such as incomplete deactivation of canister interior surfaces resulting in active sites for adsorption or decomposition.

steps.text: Be cognizant of compounds that interfere with target analytes when operating in MS modes that do not provide full-scan ion spectra (i.e., selected ion monitoring [SIM] and selected ion storage [SIS]).
Use specially prepared canisters at least 6 liters in volume that are suitable for trace gas analysis of the target compounds, ensuring they meet the requirements in Section 8.3.
Use canisters with valves designed specifically for trace level measurements.

constraints.text: Carbon dioxide in the collected sample can coelute with more volatile VOCs eluting early in the GC-MS run and interfere with their quantitation.

Artifacts in chromatograms, such as silanol compounds formed from the breakdown of silicon-ceramic linings of canisters and siloxane compounds from the breakdown of the stationary phase in an analytical column, can interfere with identification and quantitation of less volatile VOCs.

This method does not address all the safety concerns associated with its use. It is the responsibility of the user of this standard to establish appropriate field and laboratory safety and health practices prior to use.
Canisters must be able to withstand numerous cycles of evacuation to high vacuum of 0.0067 kPa (0.002 in. Hg) and pressurization to 377 kPa (40 pounds per square inch gauge, psig).

The wetted portions of the valve must, at a minimum, be constructed of chromatographic-grade stainless steel (preferably type 316), and the valve seal surfaces must be metal to metal to minimize absorption and off-gassing of VOCs and other potential contaminants.

steps.text: Recalibrate the gas dilution system once per calendar year using NIST-traceable primary flow standards with an uncertainty ≤ 0.25%.
Report the results of the calibration, listing the date of the most recent calibration and the due date.

constraints.text: Operate with an acquisition rate such that at least 12 measurements are performed over a typical chromatographic peak.
Quadrupole and ion trap systems employing electron impact (EI) ionization mode must provide nominal 70 volt (V) electron energy in EI mode.
The gas dilution system must produce calibration gases whose measured values are within ± 2% of the predicted values.
Connection tubing for the dynamic dilution system must be constructed of chromatographic-grade or silicon-ceramic–coated stainless steel.
Mixing chambers or manifolds must be constructed of chromatographic-grade or silicon-ceramic–coated stainless steel, borosilicate, or quartz glass.

steps.text: Record the average flow rate during this test.
Maintain the results as part of a laboratory record associated with the sampling device.
Assemble an evacuated canister and the sampling device connected to a certified flow meter in the same manner used for the flow control verification test.
Open the evacuated canister.
Allow sufficient time for the system to stabilize.
Record the flowrate upstream of the flow control device.
Collect two additional flow rate measurements.
Calculate the average flowrate.
Adjust the controller and repeat the flow check.
Install the sampling device on an evacuated canister equipped with a MFCD and tightly cap the inlet to the sampling device.
Open the canister valve fully.
Re-close the valve.
Observe the vacuum/pressure gauge for a minimum of 2 minutes.

preconditions.text: Prior to and after each field sampling event
If the flow rate has changed and is outside the desired range

constraints.text: The flow control verification test is considered acceptable when the sampling apparatus maintains a constant flow rate for 24-hours and until at least 75% of the canister volume is collected, which is equivalent to approximately 28 kPa (7 in. Hg or 4 psia) below atmospheric pressure.
The flow control flow check is considered valid if within +/- 10% of the reference flow rate.
If you observe an increase in pressure, the sampling device does not qualify as leak-free.

steps.text: Reassemble and flush the sampler with humidified HCF zero air or ultrapure nitrogen for at least 12 hours.
Clean canisters and verify that the canisters meet cleanliness and vacuum criteria specified in Sections 8.3 through 8.5 of this method.
Evacuate the canisters prior to field deployment.
Verify that the canisters continue to meet vacuum threshold requirements.
Clean and verify the cleanliness and flowrates of sample devices that you will use for sampling, and ensure that a clean particulate filter is placed in the inlet of the sampling device.
Establish sample codes (unique identifiers) and develop field data page and/or chain of custody (COC)/sample collection data form(s).

Ensure you have the proper number of canisters and sampling devices for the number of samples required for the sampling location and QC samples, allowing for sufficient timing for samples to arrive at the site.
Develop a unique sampling location ID.

preconditions.text: Canisters were previously cleaned and stored under pressure while awaiting use

constraints.text: To avoid damage to deactivated stainless-steel components due to oxidation in the presence of oxygen-containing atmospheres (e.g., HCF zero air), you should not heat components treated with silicon-ceramic coatings above 80 °C unless evacuated or under an inert atmosphere (e.g., nitrogen).
The sampling location must meet any requirements set in the applicable regulation and be in a secure location that protects the canister and sampling inlet from unwanted tampering or damage.

steps.text: Verify that each canister has been cleaned and blanked within the last 30 days.
Label each canister with a sample ID code and record the canister and sample ID on the field data page.
Verify the sample device is in working order and calibrate/verify the flow rate setting, if applicable, with a reference flow meter.
Record the sample device ID, expected flowrate, and the reference flowrate if calibrated/verified in the field, including the reference flow meter if applicable.
Attach the sampling device to the canister and locate at the appropriate sampling location.
Record the sampling location ID, latitude, longitude, date, and time that you installed the canister on the field data page.
Measure and record the canister vacuum using the field pressure measurement gauge, and verify that the canister has not leaked and has sufficient vacuum to collect the sample.
Conduct leak checks as specified in Section 8.3.2 of this method and record the results on the field data page.
Open the canister valve.
Record the date and time that you opened the valve as the start time, and record the initial canister vacuum/pressure and any other comments on the field data page.
Sample for the period as defined in the applicable standard.

constraints.text: You must not use a canister for sampling that has not been cleaned and blanked within 30 days of sampling.
You must replace the canister if the initial pressure is not within -1 in. Hg absolute zero (-3.39 kPa or -0.5 psi).

steps.text: Record the initial canister vacuum/pressure and any other comments such as unusual events or conditions that may impact sample results on the field data page.
Sample for the period as defined in the applicable standard (e.g., 24 hours +/- 1 hour).
At the end of the sampling period, close the valve.
Record the date and time that you closed the valve as the end time.
Remove the sampling device and attach the field pressure measurement gauge.
Open the canister valve and measure and record the final canister vacuum/pressure and any other comments such as unusual events or conditions that may impact sample results on the field data page.
Flag the canister.
Disconnect the field canister pressure measurement gauge and replace with a cap.
Verify the sample device is still in working order and verify the flow rate setting with a reference flow meter.
Record the final flowrate on the field data page.
Collect a separate co-located sample for at least one sampling location using a discrete MFCD.
Collect canister field blanks.
Prepare a canister blank by filling a canister with humidified clean diluent gas to approximately -15 in. Hg ± 1 in. Hg.
Record the pressure and transport to the field site(s) to accompany field-collected canisters.

preconditions.text: If final pressure is greater than -3 in. Hg gauge pressure (-10.2 kPag or -1.5 psig)

constraints.text: The collection of the field duplicates must follow the same procedure and occur at the same time as the co-located field sample.
Canister field blanks are to be treated identically to field-collected samples in the field and laboratory including pressure checks, MFCD leak checks, etc.

steps.text: Analyze an IB at the beginning of the sequence and prior to analysis of the ICAL standard and daily CCV standard.
Analyze a laboratory MB prior to and following the ICAL in an ICAL sequence and prior to analyzing the CCV standard.
Analyze the canister field blank as part of the same analytical sequence as the accompanying field samples.
Analyze the CB when the ICAL is established and when preparing any new CCV standard using the same instrument method that was used for standards and field samples when establishing the ICAL.
Analyze and report duplicate samples as part of this method.
Analyze the field duplicate during the same analytical sequence as the accompanying field sample.

preconditions.text: Ensure the volume used for analysis of blanks matches the volume of sample to be analyzed (unless otherwise stated).

constraints.text: Analysis of all blanks must demonstrate each target compound is < 20 pptv 14.7 psia or undetected (whichever is more stringent) per compound of interest.

The MB must consist of a canister filled with humidified (40 to 50% RH) clean diluent gas (the same diluent used for sample dilution) and analyzed via the same instrument method as the standards and field samples.

The CB must consist of a canister filled with the humidified (40 to 50% RH) clean diluent gas sourced through the dilution system employed to prepare standards (or gas used to dilute the calibration standard if dynamic/automated static dilution is not used).
The RPD of the precision measurements should agree within ± 30%.

steps.text: Connect a pressure transducer or gauge to an evacuated canister to monitor the canister pressure as you add gases.
Add stock and diluent gases separately through a manifold or by direct connection of the gas to the standard canister or vessel.
Measure the canister pressure before and after standard and diluent gases are bled into the canister, and input these pressures into the calculation of the dilution factor and final concentrations.
Calculate the final concentration of each target compound in the diluted standard using Equation 8 in Section 12 of this method.
Store standards prepared in canisters at ambient laboratory conditions in locations that are free of potential contaminants for up to 7 days.
Condition preconcentrator traps when first installed to eliminate contaminants that act as interferences or chromatographic artifacts, per manufacturer recommendation.
Analyze the IBs and MBs to verify the preconcentrator system meets the method criteria.
Optimize GC-MS System.

preconditions.text: After the recommended conditioning procedure is completed

constraints.text: The gas dilution system shall produce calibration gases whose measured values are within ± 2% of the predicted values.
The pressure transducer or gauge must meet the requirements in Section 6.5 of this method.
For preconcentrator traps that contain multiple types of sorbent beds, the oven temperature must not exceed the lowest conditioning temperature of the sorbents contained in the trap.

steps.text: Optimize GC conditions for compound separation and sensitivity by establishing GC carrier gas flow rates, oven temperature program, and instrument run time based on the manufacturer’s recommendations and customize as needed.
Tune/optimize the MS (quadrupole, ion trap, or TOF MS) to demonstrate acceptable performance across the selected ion mass range according to the manufacturer’s specifications.
Conduct a BFB tuning check for linear quadrupole or ion trap MS instruments.

Introduce 1 to 2 ng of BFB into the preconcentrator and analyze the standard using the preconcentrator parameters established and used for the analysis of calibration standards, QC samples, and field samples.
Use the method integration and analysis parameters employed for routine analysis of standards, QC samples, and field samples.

preconditions.text: Upon initial installation of the instrument and following significant preventive maintenance or repair activities that impact the performance of the GC-MS system.
Before the ICAL and at least once during every 24-hour period of analyzing samples, blanks, or calibration standards thereafter.

constraints.text: For preconcentrator traps that contain multiple types of sorbent beds, the oven temperature must not exceed the lowest conditioning temperature of the sorbents contained in the trap.

The BFB tuning check must show that the GC-MS system meets the mass spectral ion abundance criteria listed in Table 2 in Section 17 of this method for the target compounds before you can use the system for any analysis.

steps.text: Adjust the tuning of the MS or take corrective actions.
Select IS compound(s) to be used for oHAP analysis.

Evaluate the IS upon receipt for the presence of contaminants by analyzing increasing volumes of the IS and examining the results for compound contaminants whose responses increase proportionally with the increasing volume of IS analyzed.
Add the IS through a dedicated non-sample port in the preconcentrator at the same concentration for each injection.

Choose the concentration of IS added to each injection such that the peak area response for the IS compound approximates the area responses for target compounds in the lower half of the calibration curve range, but that minimally provides a peak that is on scale and does not exceed the area response of the highest calibration standard.

preconditions.text: If the GC-MS system cannot meet the BFB tuning criteria
If using purchased IS stock gases

constraints.text: The BFB tuning check must show that the GC-MS system meets the mass spectral ion abundance criteria listed in Table 2 in Section 17 of this method for the target compounds before you can use the system for any analysis.
You must not use this system until the abundance criteria has been met.
Do not use IS gas standards that fail the MB acceptance criteria.

steps.text: Report results in International System of Units (SI units).

preconditions.text: The regulatory authority for testing does not specify English units.

steps.text: Calculate Canister Final Air/Nitrogen Volume (Vcalc) using Equation 1.
Calculate Acceptable Concentration Criterion (Cacc) using Equation 2.
Calculate Percent Difference of the Measured Concentration of Each Target Compound in the CCV Standard (%DCCV) from the Theoretical Concentration using Equation 3.
Calculate Percent Recovery (%RecoveryCCV) using Equation 4.
Calculate Relative Percent Difference (RPD) using Equation 5.
Calculate Water Volume to Add to Canister (Vw) using Equation 6.
Calculate Final Concentration of the Diluted Standard (Cf) – Dynamic Dilution using Equation 7.

preconditions.text: If you combine multiple gas standards for dilution, the equation denominator is the sum of all gas flows combined for preparing the dilution.

constraints.text: The equation assumes the density of water to be 1 g/mL and that 100% of the added water to the canister is in the gas phase. The equation does not correct the density of water for the ambient temperature.

steps.text: Pre-evacuate the canister.
Set canister temperature to 80 °C.
Set minimum air purge gas humidity to 50%.
Run a minimum of 5 pressure/evacuation cycles (cycle time varies by system).
